title: Predicting Groundwater Depletion Through Physics-Informed GeoAI and Time-Series Satellite Data
brief_rationale: Integrating hydrological physical constraints into a machine learning model, this study improves the accuracy of long-term water security forecasts in arid regions.
impact_evidence: Groundwater depletion threatens global food security and ecological stability, particularly in arid regions. By integrating physical hydrological constraints into deep learning models, this research provides highly accurate, physically plausible, and scalable groundwater forecasts. These improved predictions are critical for policymakers, agricultural stakeholders, and water resource managers to design sustainable extraction limits and climate adaptation strategies without relying on computationally expensive traditional hydrological models.
publishability: Water Resources Research (focuses on hydrology and water resource management); Remote Sensing of Environment (ideal for studies leveraging satellite time-series data like GRACE); Computers & Geosciences (fits the GeoAI and physics-informed machine learning methodology).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Spatial grid cells (0.25 degree by 0.25 degree resolution) over major arid aquifers (e.g., High Plains Aquifer, Central Valley) at a monthly temporal scale over a 20-year period (2003-2023).
outcomes: Groundwater Storage Anomaly (GWSA) measured in cm equivalent water thickness
Predicted future GWSA at 1-month, 3-month, and 6-month lead times
exposures: Precipitation
Evapotranspiration
Soil moisture
Land surface temperature
Vegetation indices (NDVI)
Historical GRACE Terrestrial Water Storage (TWS) anomalies
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: The estimand is the predictive mapping function (conditional expectation) of future groundwater storage given historical satellite inputs, constrained by the physical water balance equation (Change in Storage = Precipitation - Evapotranspiration - Runoff - Groundwater Draft). The strategy involves training a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) where the loss function explicitly penalizes predictions that violate this mass conservation principle.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) / Spatiotemporal Deep Learning (e.g., Physics-guided ConvLSTM)
robustness_checks: Varying the weight of the physics-loss penalty term in the objective function to assess trade-offs between data fit and physical consistency.
Temporal cross-validation by training on 2003-2015 data and testing on 2016-2023 data to evaluate forecasting stability.
Spatial cross-validation (leave-one-region-out) to test the spatial generalizability of the model across different aquifer sub-basins.
Ablation study removing specific input variables (e.g., NDVI, soil moisture) to determine feature importance.
Comparison against purely data-driven baseline models (standard LSTM, Random Forest) to quantify the value added by the physics constraint.
Comparison against traditional hydrological models (e.g., GLDAS outputs) to benchmark computational efficiency and accuracy.
Sensitivity analysis to different spatial resolutions by aggregating data to 0.5 degree by 0.5 degree grids.
Adding synthetic Gaussian noise to satellite inputs to test model stability and robustness to measurement errors.
expected_tables_figures: Table of model performance metrics (RMSE, MAE, NSE, R-squared) comparing the PINN against baseline ML and traditional models.
Time-series plots of predicted versus observed GWSA for specific high-depletion grid cells.
Spatial maps showing the distribution of prediction errors and groundwater depletion trends across the aquifer.
Figure illustrating the architecture of the Physics-Informed GeoAI model and the integration of the water balance loss function.
Table of ablation study results and physics-loss weight sensitivity analysis.

data_sources.name: NASA GRACE and GRACE-FO (Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment)
data_sources.accessibility: Provides Terrestrial Water Storage (TWS) anomalies. Freely accessible to the public via NASA Earthdata and the JPL PO.DAAC portal without paywalls.

data_sources.name: GLDAS (Global Land Data Assimilation System)
data_sources.accessibility: Provides soil moisture, snow water equivalent, and canopy water data needed to isolate groundwater from TWS. Freely accessible via NASA GES DISC.

data_sources.name: CHIRPS (Climate Hazards Group InfraRed Precipitation with Station data)
data_sources.accessibility: Provides high-resolution precipitation data. Freely accessible via the UC Santa Barbara Climate Hazards Center and Google Earth Engine.

data_sources.name: MODIS (Terra/Aqua)
data_sources.accessibility: Provides Evapotranspiration (MOD16) and NDVI (MOD13) products. Freely accessible via USGS EarthExplorer and Google Earth Engine.

title: Dynamic Cartography of Post-Conflict Reconstruction: A Multi-Source GeoAI Analysis
brief_rationale: This study uses night-time light intensity and high-resolution optical imagery to track the quantitative progress of economic recovery in post-conflict zones.
impact_evidence: This research provides critical, high-frequency, and spatially disaggregated metrics of economic recovery in post-conflict zones, where traditional survey data is often dangerous to collect, unreliable, or non-existent. The findings will directly aid international development organizations (e.g., World Bank, USAID, UN) in optimizing the spatial allocation of reconstruction funds, monitoring the efficacy of aid in real-time, and identifying lagging regions to prevent conflict recidivism.
publishability: 1. Remote Sensing of Environment (high impact for novel satellite data applications); 2. Computers, Environment and Urban Systems (strong fit for GeoAI and urban reconstruction); 3. Journal of Peace Research (excellent for quantitative conflict/post-conflict studies).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: 1km x 1km spatial grid cells aggregated to a monthly panel over a 10-year period (e.g., 2014-2024) in selected post-conflict regions (e.g., Colombia, Iraq, or Syria).
outcomes: Monthly Night-Time Light (NTL) radiance (proxy for economic activity)
Built-up area index / Building footprint density (derived from Sentinel-2 optical imagery using deep learning segmentation)
exposures: Post-conflict status (binary indicator, 1 for months following localized conflict cessation)
Cumulative months of localized peace
Proximity to major reconstruction aid hubs or infrastructure nodes
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) of localized conflict cessation on economic recovery. The identification strategy relies on a staggered Difference-in-Differences (DiD) or Event Study design, leveraging the spatial and temporal variation in the cessation of armed conflict across different grid cells.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Staggered Difference-in-Differences (e.g., Callaway & Sant'Anna estimator) with grid-cell and year-month fixed effects.
robustness_checks: Implementation of alternative staggered DiD estimators (e.g., Sun & Abraham, Borusyak et al.) to correct for negative weighting in TWFE.
Estimation using Conley spatial standard errors to account for spatial autocorrelation across neighboring grid cells.
Placebo tests using randomized conflict cessation dates to test for spurious pre-trends.
Exclusion of capital cities and major metropolitan areas to ensure results are not driven solely by primary urban centers.
Sensitivity analysis to the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) by re-aggregating the unit of analysis to 5km x 5km and administrative level 2 boundaries.
Controlling for seasonal environmental confounders (e.g., cloud cover, snow albedo) and static geographic features (elevation, slope).
expected_tables_figures: Table 1: Summary statistics of conflict events, NTL radiance, and built-up indices.
Figure 1: High-resolution spatial maps comparing pre-conflict, peak-conflict, and post-conflict NTL and optical imagery for selected micro-regions.
Figure 2: Event study coefficient plot showing the dynamic treatment effects of conflict cessation on NTL radiance (testing parallel trends).
Table 2: Main staggered DiD regression results for both NTL and built-up area outcomes.
Figure 3: Heterogeneity analysis plot (e.g., recovery rates by distance to capital or initial conflict intensity).
Table 3: Robustness checks matrix displaying coefficients across the 6+ alternative specifications.

data_sources.name: VIIRS Day/Night Band (DNB) Nighttime Lights
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available via the Earth Observation Group (EOG) and Google Earth Engine (GEE) without paywalls, providing monthly cloud-free composites.

data_sources.name: Sentinel-2 Multispectral Instrument (MSI) Level-2A
data_sources.accessibility: Open access via the European Space Agency (ESA) Copernicus Open Access Hub and Google Earth Engine, providing 10m resolution optical imagery globally.

data_sources.name: Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project (ACLED)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely accessible for academic and non-commercial research via their public API, providing geocoded, daily data on political violence and conflict events.

title: A GeoAI Approach to Assessing the Impact of Renewable Energy Infrastructure on Local Biodiversity
brief_rationale: Integrating species distribution models with spatial energy infrastructure data to identify optimization strategies that minimize ecological fragmentation.
impact_evidence: As renewable energy scales globally to meet climate targets, its spatial footprint increasingly overlaps with critical habitats. This research provides actionable, data-driven insights for policymakers and energy developers to optimize site selection, minimizing ecological fragmentation. It aligns with the UN SDGs (Affordable and Clean Energy, Life on Land) and addresses a critical trade-off in sustainability planning.
publishability: Remote Sensing of Environment (for the GeoAI and satellite imagery methodology), Applied Energy (for the energy infrastructure focus), Landscape Ecology (for the biodiversity and spatial fragmentation aspect).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: 1km x 1km spatial grid cells across the contiguous United States over a 15-year period (panel data).
outcomes: Habitat fragmentation index (effective mesh size)
Species richness index (derived from species distribution models)
Vegetation index anomalies (NDVI/EVI degradation)
exposures: Presence of renewable energy infrastructure (binary indicator for wind turbines or solar arrays)
Density of renewable energy infrastructure (megawatts per square kilometer)
Proximity to nearest renewable energy installation (kilometers)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) of renewable infrastructure installation on biodiversity metrics. The identification strategy relies on a Difference-in-Differences (DiD) design with staggered adoption, utilizing spatial propensity score matching to compare grid cells that received infrastructure with observationally similar control cells.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Two-way Fixed Effects (TWFE) Panel Regression and Staggered Difference-in-Differences (e.g., Callaway and Sant'Anna estimator) with spatial error adjustments.
robustness_checks: Alternative spatial resolutions (e.g., aggregating to 5km x 5km grids)
Placebo tests using future installation dates to test for pre-treatment parallel trends
Spatially clustered standard errors (Conley standard errors) to account for spatial autocorrelation
Exclusion of grid cells with overlapping urban development or agriculture to isolate renewable energy effects
Alternative biodiversity metrics (Shannon diversity index instead of simple species richness)
Propensity score matching with varying caliper widths for control group selection
Leave-one-region-out cross-validation to ensure results are not driven by a single geographic area
expected_tables_figures: Summary statistics table of grid cell characteristics (treated vs. control)
Event-study plot showing pre- and post-treatment effects on biodiversity indices to verify parallel trends
Main regression results table (DiD estimates with various fixed effects and controls)
Spatial map of the study area highlighting treatment cells and estimated localized impact magnitudes
Robustness checks table showing sensitivity to varying spatial scales and standard error clustering

data_sources.name: US Wind Turbine Database (USWTDB) and Global Solar Power Tracker
data_sources.accessibility: Publicly available datasets with direct CSV/GeoJSON downloads and no paywall, providing exact coordinates and commissioning dates.

data_sources.name: Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF)
data_sources.accessibility: Open-access API providing millions of species occurrence records globally, freely available for research.

data_sources.name: Google Earth Engine (GEE) Data Catalog (Landsat/Sentinel-2)
data_sources.accessibility: Free access for research purposes to compute NDVI, EVI, and land cover fragmentation metrics at scale.

data_sources.name: WorldClim
data_sources.accessibility: Free download of high-resolution global climate data (temperature, precipitation) used for control variables in spatial matching.

title: Urban Heat Islands and Mental Health: A Quantitative Study of Temperature and Mental Health Crisis Calls
brief_rationale: By correlating hyper-local temperature data with 911 mental health crisis call logs, this study examines the impact of extreme heat on acute mental health incidents.
impact_evidence: Extreme heat is a rapidly growing public health threat due to climate change. While the physiological impacts of heatwaves are well-documented, the acute psychiatric impacts remain understudied. Quantifying the relationship between hyper-local temperature spikes and mental health crises provides actionable intelligence for emergency dispatch resource allocation, urban planning (e.g., targeted cooling centers, green space deployment), and public health interventions during extreme weather events.
publishability: 1. Environmental Health Perspectives (ideal for environmental exposures and health outcomes); 2. Journal of Urban Health (strong fit for urban environments, spatial disparities, and public health); 3. Social Science & Medicine (suitable for the intersection of environmental stressors and societal health outcomes).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Census tract-day level over a multi-year warm season period (e.g., May-September, 2015-2023) in a major US city (e.g., New York City or Chicago).
outcomes: Daily count of 911 calls coded for mental health crises/psychiatric emergencies per census tract
Rate of mental health 911 calls per 10,000 residents per tract-day
exposures: Daily maximum temperature (°C/°F) at the tract level
Heatwave indicator (binary variable for ≥3 consecutive days above the 95th percentile of the historical baseline)
Urban Heat Island (UHI) intensity index (land surface temperature deviation from regional mean)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Marginal effect of temperature on the incidence of mental health crisis calls. The identification strategy relies on the quasi-random day-to-day variation in local weather. A Two-Way Fixed Effects (TWFE) approach is used to control for time-invariant spatial confounders (tract fixed effects) and city-wide temporal shocks (date fixed effects).
quantitative_specs.model_family: Poisson or Negative Binomial Fixed-Effects Regression (for overdispersed count data) with clustered standard errors at the census tract level.
robustness_checks: Alternative temperature thresholds (e.g., testing 90th, 95th, and 99th percentiles for heatwave definitions).
Placebo tests using non-weather-sensitive 911 calls (e.g., indoor plumbing issues, non-acute administrative calls) to rule out general dispatch volume artifacts.
Inclusion of daily air pollution controls (PM2.5, Ozone) to isolate the temperature effect from correlated poor air quality.
Distributed Lag Non-linear Models (DLNM) to check for delayed effects (lags up to 7 days) and potential harvesting (displacement) effects.
Sub-sample heterogeneity analysis by neighborhood income, racial composition, and baseline tree canopy cover to test for differential vulnerability.
Alternative spatial aggregation (ZIP code vs. Census Tract) to check for the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP).
Controlling for day-of-week, month, and holiday fixed effects to account for temporal behavioral patterns.
expected_tables_figures: Table 1: Summary statistics of 911 calls, temperature, and demographics by census tract.
Table 2: Main regression results (baseline model, adding sequential controls, and different temperature metrics).
Figure 1: Non-linear exposure-response curve showing daily maximum temperature vs. relative risk of a mental health call.
Figure 2: Spatial map of UHI intensity overlaid with mental health call hotspots.
Figure 3: Forest plot showing effect size heterogeneity across different socioeconomic and environmental strata (e.g., high vs. low green space).

data_sources.name: City Open Data Portals (e.g., NYC Open Data - 911 Incident Response Data)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available via API and CSV downloads. Municipalities like NYC, Chicago, and Seattle provide historical, geocoded 911 dispatch data updated daily without paywalls or manual approval.

data_sources.name: PRISM Climate Group / Daymet (NASA/ORNL)
data_sources.accessibility: High-resolution (1km) daily gridded weather data (temperature, precipitation) freely accessible via API, FTP, and Google Earth Engine for academic and public use.

data_sources.name: US Census Bureau (American Community Survey)
data_sources.accessibility: Free API access for demographic, socioeconomic, and housing control variables at the census tract level.

data_sources.name: EPA EJScreen
data_sources.accessibility: Open access spatial datasets providing environmental justice metrics, including baseline air toxics and green space indicators, available for direct download.

title: The Longitudinal Association Between Urban Heat Island Intensity and Emergency Medical Service Call Volumes
brief_rationale: This study correlates local temperature sensor data with open-source EMS incident reports to determine if hyper-local heat spikes predict acute medical crises.
impact_evidence: This research provides actionable insights for municipal emergency management and urban planning by quantifying the exact strain hyper-local heat spikes place on healthcare infrastructure. Findings can directly inform dynamic EMS dispatch routing, targeted cooling center deployments, and long-term climate adaptation policies such as urban greening in highly vulnerable neighborhoods.
publishability: 1. Environmental Health Perspectives (strong fit for climate-health epidemiology); 2. Journal of Urban Health (ideal for intersection of urban environments and public health); 3. Sustainable Cities and Society (suitable for the urban informatics and infrastructure implications).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Census tract-day (or 1km x 1km grid cell-day) over a multi-year period (e.g., 5-10 years) during warm season months (May-September).
outcomes: Daily count of total EMS dispatches per tract
Daily count of heat-related EMS dispatches (e.g., heat stroke, dehydration)
Daily count of cardiovascular and respiratory-related EMS dispatches
exposures: Daily maximum Urban Heat Island (UHI) intensity (local tract temperature minus regional rural baseline)
Daily maximum absolute temperature
Duration of heatwave (consecutive days above 90th percentile local temperature)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Average Marginal Effect (AME) of a 1-degree Celsius increase in local UHI intensity on the incidence rate of EMS calls. The identification strategy relies on quasi-random daily within-tract variation in weather, utilizing a panel design with high-dimensional fixed effects to absorb time-invariant neighborhood characteristics and city-wide temporal shocks.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Fixed-Effects Poisson or Negative Binomial Regression (with Distributed Lag Non-linear Models - DLNM - to capture delayed effects).
robustness_checks: Alternative UHI measurement: Using nighttime Land Surface Temperature (LST) vs. daytime LST.
Placebo outcomes: Estimating the effect of UHI intensity on unrelated EMS calls (e.g., traffic accidents, trauma).
Distributed lag models: Testing for mortality/morbidity displacement (harvesting effect) over a 7- to 14-day lag window.
Confounder adjustment: Adding daily local air pollution metrics (PM2.5, Ozone) to isolate the temperature effect.
Spatial sensitivity: Re-aggregating the data to ZIP code or neighborhood tabulation areas instead of census tracts.
Standard error clustering: Utilizing two-way clustered standard errors at the census tract and date levels to account for spatial and temporal autocorrelation.
Heterogeneity analysis: Stratifying results by neighborhood socioeconomic status (SES), redlining history, and baseline tree canopy cover.
expected_tables_figures: Table 1: Summary statistics of EMS calls, UHI intensity, and neighborhood demographics.
Table 2: Main regression results showing Incidence Rate Ratios (IRR) for total and cause-specific EMS calls.
Figure 1: Bivariate choropleth map showing average summer UHI intensity overlaid with EMS call hotspots.
Figure 2: Distributed lag non-linear model (DLNM) plot showing the delayed effects of a heat spike over 14 days.
Figure 3: Forest plot of heterogeneous effects stratified by neighborhood income, age demographics, and green space.

data_sources.name: City Open Data Portals (e.g., NYC Open Data, Chicago Data Portal, Austin Texas Open Data)
data_sources.accessibility: Provides freely accessible, anonymized, and geocoded historical EMS dispatch records via API or bulk CSV downloads without paywalls.

data_sources.name: Google Earth Engine (MODIS / Landsat 8 LST)
data_sources.accessibility: Offers free, programmatic access to high-resolution satellite imagery to calculate daily or 8-day composite Land Surface Temperature (LST) for UHI derivation.

data_sources.name: US Census Bureau API (American Community Survey)
data_sources.accessibility: Publicly available API providing free access to tract-level demographic and socioeconomic variables (e.g., age, income, race) for vulnerability stratification.

data_sources.name: EPA Air Quality System (AQS) API
data_sources.accessibility: Freely accessible API providing daily station-level air pollution data (PM2.5, Ozone) required for robustness checks.

title: Causal Discovery in Spatiotemporal Climate Models: Identifying Tipping Points via Transformer-Based Architectures
brief_rationale: This research utilizes causal inference and transformer models on open-access ERA5 climate data to move beyond predictive correlation toward identifying causal triggers in environmental systemic collapse.
impact_evidence: By shifting from predictive correlation to causal discovery, this research provides actionable intelligence for climate policy and early warning systems. Identifying the exact causal triggers of environmental tipping points allows policymakers to target interventions more effectively, directly supporting IPCC objectives and global mitigation strategies.
publishability: Nature Climate Change (for high-impact climate findings); Remote Sensing of Environment (for spatial data applications); IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing (for the methodological AI/Transformer advancements).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Spatiotemporal grid cells (e.g., 0.25 degree resolution) at monthly and daily time steps spanning 1979 to present.
outcomes: Abrupt shifts in vegetation indices (e.g., NDVI anomalies)
Sea ice concentration collapse indicators
Localized extreme temperature/precipitation regime shifts
exposures: Prolonged drought indices (e.g., SPEI)
Cumulative heat stress metrics
Regional greenhouse gas concentration proxies
Deforestation and land-use change rates
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Average Treatment Effect (ATE) of extreme climate anomalies on ecosystem collapse indicators, identified via a causal transformer architecture utilizing self-attention weights to map spatiotemporal Granger causality and structural causal models in latent space.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Causal Transformer Models (e.g., Attention-based Structural Causal Models, Spatiotemporal Granger Causality Networks)
robustness_checks: Alternative spatial resolutions (e.g., 0.5 degree vs 0.25 degree grid cells).
Temporal window sensitivity analysis by varying the lag length in the transformer attention mechanism.
Placebo tests randomizing spatial coordinates to check for spurious causal links.
Alternative tipping point thresholds (e.g., 90th vs 95th percentile anomalies).
Sub-sample analysis by distinct climate zones (e.g., Tropics vs. Arctic).
Algorithmic comparison with traditional causal discovery methods like the PC algorithm and convergent cross mapping.
expected_tables_figures: Attention map visualizations illustrating spatiotemporal causal pathways.
Table comparing identified causal links against baseline predictive correlations.
Time-series plots of predicted versus actual tipping point occurrences.
Robustness check summary table detailing metric stability across specifications.

data_sources.name: ERA5 (ECMWF via Copernicus Climate Data Store)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely accessible open-access climate reanalysis dataset with a public API, requiring only a free registration.

data_sources.name: MODIS/Landsat via Google Earth Engine
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available satellite imagery and derived indices (like NDVI) accessible via the public GEE catalog and API.

title: Global Mapping of Informal Waste Dumping Sites using Satellite Imagery and Weakly-Supervised Learning
brief_rationale: This project addresses the global lack of standardized waste site data by applying weakly-supervised learning to classify dumpsites across diverse landscapes, aiding in environmental health policy.
impact_evidence: This research provides critical, scalable data for environmental health policy, urban planning, and global sustainability tracking (SDG 11 and 12). By automating the detection of informal waste sites, it enables municipalities and NGOs to target cleanup efforts, track illegal dumping over time, and assess environmental justice implications. Academically, it advances GeoAI by demonstrating the viability of weakly-supervised learning in highly heterogeneous, globally distributed remote sensing tasks where labeled data is scarce.
publishability: 1. Remote Sensing of Environment (High impact for novel remote sensing applications); 2. ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing (Ideal for computer vision applied to earth observation); 3. Computers, Environment and Urban Systems (Strong fit for urban policy and spatial analysis implications).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Spatial image patches (e.g., 256x256 pixels at 10m resolution) and corresponding grid cells across selected global metropolitan and peri-urban areas over the 2020-2023 period.
outcomes: Probability score of informal waste dumping site presence
Estimated spatial extent (bounding box or segmentation mask) of the dumpsite
exposures: Weak labels derived from open-source point data (e.g., OpenStreetMap landuse=landfill tags)
Multi-spectral satellite imagery features (Sentinel-2 RGB, NIR, SWIR bands, NDVI)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: The estimand is the predictive accuracy and spatial generalization of the weakly-supervised model compared to fully supervised baselines. The strategy involves training a Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) or Class Activation Mapping (CAM) based Convolutional Neural Network using image-level weak labels to predict pixel-level dumpsite presence, evaluated via spatial cross-validation.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Weakly-Supervised Convolutional Neural Networks (e.g., ResNet/EfficientNet with Grad-CAM) and Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) frameworks.
robustness_checks: Spatial block cross-validation (leave-one-region-out) to test geographic generalization across different continents.
Sensitivity analysis to varying levels of label noise in the weak training data.
Comparison against traditional fully-supervised baselines using a small, hand-labeled holdout set.
Temporal robustness check applying the model to imagery from different seasons to control for phenology bias.
Alternative backbone architectures (e.g., switching from ResNet50 to Vision Transformers).
Ablation study on input features (e.g., RGB only vs. full multi-spectral Sentinel-2 bands).
Threshold sensitivity analysis for converting continuous probability maps to binary classification masks.
expected_tables_figures: Table of classification metrics (Precision, Recall, F1-score, IoU) across different geographic regions.
Figure showing Class Activation Maps (CAMs) highlighting waste sites compared to ground truth.
Table of ablation study results comparing feature sets and architectures.
Figure mapping the spatial distribution of predicted informal waste sites in a selected case study city.
Robustness check table showing performance degradation under simulated label noise.

data_sources.name: Sentinel-2 (via Google Earth Engine)
data_sources.accessibility: Provides freely accessible, global 10m resolution multi-spectral imagery, accessible via the GEE API without paywalls for research purposes.

data_sources.name: OpenStreetMap (OSM)
data_sources.accessibility: Provides crowdsourced, freely accessible point and polygon data for waste sites, landfills, and infrastructure, easily accessible via the Overpass API.

data_sources.name: Global Human Settlement Layer (GHSL)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available from the European Commission, providing open spatial data on human presence to stratify sampling by urban and peri-urban density.

title: Detecting Illegal Small-Scale Mining Operations via Deep Change Detection and Temporal Contextualization
brief_rationale: Using high-cadence Sentinel-2 imagery and LSTM-CNN hybrids, this project monitors rapid, small-scale deforestation and excavation patterns, filling the gap in automated environmental crime detection.
impact_evidence: Illegal small-scale mining drives severe environmental degradation, deforestation, and water pollution. Automating its detection using open-source satellite imagery provides actionable, near real-time intelligence for environmental protection agencies, NGOs, and policymakers, significantly reducing the latency between illegal activity onset and regulatory intervention.
publishability: Remote Sensing of Environment (high impact for novel RS methods applied to ecology), ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing (strong fit for deep learning and computer vision in photogrammetry), Computers & Geosciences (focus on computational algorithms in earth sciences).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: 10m x 10m to 100m x 100m spatial grid patches over bi-weekly time steps across a defined high-risk region (e.g., Amazon basin or West Africa).
outcomes: Binary indicator of new mining activity onset (1 = mining detected, 0 = no mining)
Continuous measure of disturbed land area (hectares)
exposures: Spatiotemporal features extracted from Sentinel-2 multispectral time-series (e.g., NDVI, NDWI, bare soil index changes)
Temporal contextual embeddings generated from the LSTM module
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Predictive accuracy and classification performance (Precision, Recall, F1-score, Intersection over Union) of the LSTM-CNN hybrid model compared to baseline models. Identification relies on supervised learning using historically annotated mining sites and spatiotemporal contrastive learning.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Spatiotemporal Deep Learning (LSTM-CNN hybrid, ConvLSTM, or U-Net with temporal attention mechanisms)
robustness_checks: Cross-validation across different geographical sub-regions to test spatial transferability.
Sensitivity analysis to cloud cover thresholds (e.g., 10% vs 30% cloud masking).
Ablation study removing the temporal (LSTM) component to isolate the value of temporal contextualization.
Performance evaluation under varying spatial resolutions (e.g., downsampling Sentinel-2 to 30m Landsat-like resolution).
Temporal window sensitivity analysis (comparing 3-month, 6-month, and 12-month historical context windows).
Placebo tests on known non-mining deforestation areas (e.g., legal logging or agriculture) to evaluate false positive rates.
expected_tables_figures: Table of classification metrics (Precision, Recall, F1, IoU) across baseline and proposed models.
Figure showing the temporal evolution of a detected mining site alongside model activation maps.
Spatial map of predicted mining hotspots overlaid on regional boundaries.
Ablation study results table demonstrating the contribution of each neural network module.
Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) and Precision-Recall (PR) curves.

data_sources.name: Copernicus Sentinel-2 (via Google Earth Engine or AWS Open Data)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available, open-access multispectral imagery with global coverage and a 5-day revisit time, accessible via public APIs without paywalls.

data_sources.name: Global Forest Watch (Tree Cover Loss data)
data_sources.accessibility: Openly accessible via API and bulk download; provides baseline deforestation data to cross-reference mining-induced forest loss.

data_sources.name: OpenStreetMap (OSM)
data_sources.accessibility: Free, crowdsourced geographic data accessible via the Overpass API, used to mask out known legal infrastructure, roads, and settlements.

domain: Inferring Causal Relationships Between Public Health and the Built Environment, Large-Scale Urban Models and Generative Urban Science, Cross-Scale Urban System Modeling and Spatial Network Analysis
title: Generative Modeling of Neighborhood Walkability and Its Impact on Type 2 Diabetes Prevalence
brief_rationale: This research utilizes generative adversarial networks to synthesize neighborhood design variations, testing their causal impact on diabetes incidence using open-access community health data and street-level imagery.
impact_evidence: This research provides actionable, scalable evidence for urban planners and public health officials by simulating the health impacts of built-environment interventions without the need for costly physical pilot projects. By quantifying how specific streetscape improvements (e.g., adding sidewalks, increasing tree canopy) causally reduce Type 2 Diabetes, the findings can directly inform municipal zoning policies, infrastructure investment prioritization, and preventative healthcare strategies.
publishability: 1. Health & Place (ideal for research at the intersection of built environment and public health outcomes). 2. Computers, Environment and Urban Systems (highly suitable for the application of GANs and large-scale urban spatial modeling). 3. Social Science & Medicine (appropriate for the causal epidemiological focus on chronic disease).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Census Tracts in major US Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs), cross-sectional analysis based on 2020-2022 data.
outcomes: Age-adjusted prevalence of diagnosed Type 2 Diabetes among adults aged >=18 years (percentage)
exposures: Baseline image-derived walkability index (extracted via computer vision from street-level imagery)
GAN-generated counterfactual walkability index (simulated high-walkability streetscape embeddings)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Average Treatment Effect (ATE) of transitioning a neighborhood from low to high walkability. The identification strategy relies on Double Machine Learning (DML) to control for high-dimensional socio-demographic and spatial confounders, utilizing the GAN to generate synthetic 'treated' counterfactual images to estimate the marginal effect of visual built-environment changes on health outcomes.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Double Machine Learning (DML) with Causal Forests, paired with a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN) for exposure synthesis.
robustness_checks: Alternative walkability metrics: Comparing image-derived walkability effects against the EPA's traditional National Walkability Index.
Spatial autocorrelation adjustments: Re-estimating standard errors using Conley spatial HAC standard errors.
Placebo outcome tests: Estimating the effect of walkability on a theoretically unrelated health outcome (e.g., genetic disorders or hearing loss prevalence).
Sub-sample heterogeneity: Stratifying the analysis by climate zones (e.g., Sunbelt vs. Rustbelt cities) to account for weather-related walking barriers.
Dose-response sensitivity: Varying the magnitude of the GAN latent space perturbation to test if larger synthetic walkability improvements yield proportionally larger health benefits.

Gentrification and spatial sorting controls: Including proxies for neighborhood change (e.g., 5-year change in median rent, housing burden) to isolate built-environment effects from demographic displacement.
Alternative confounding adjustment: Comparing DML estimates with traditional Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighting (IPTW).
expected_tables_figures: Table 1: Summary statistics of census tracts (demographics, baseline diabetes prevalence, baseline walkability scores).
Figure 1: Visual grid of original street-level images paired with their GAN-generated 'high walkability' counterfactuals.
Table 2: Main causal estimates (ATE) of walkability improvements on Type 2 Diabetes prevalence.
Figure 2: Spatial heat map of a selected MSA showing predicted absolute reductions in diabetes prevalence under the synthetic intervention scenario.
Figure 3: Dose-response curve illustrating the relationship between the intensity of synthetic walkability features (e.g., green space ratio, sidewalk width) and the estimated reduction in diabetes.
Table 3: Robustness checks, including placebo tests, spatial standard errors, and alternative model specifications.

data_sources.name: CDC PLACES (Population Level Analysis and Community Estimates)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available via the CDC's open data portal; provides census-tract level estimates for chronic disease outcomes, including diabetes, without paywalls or data use agreements.

data_sources.name: Mapillary Street-Level Imagery
data_sources.accessibility: An open-source, crowdsourced platform for street-level imagery. The API allows free access to image data and metadata for academic research, serving as an accessible alternative to Google Street View.

data_sources.name: US Census Bureau American Community Survey (ACS) 5-Year Estimates
data_sources.accessibility: Publicly accessible via the Census API; provides comprehensive socio-demographic confounding variables (income, race, education, age) at the census tract level.

data_sources.name: EPA Smart Location Database
data_sources.accessibility: Freely downloadable from the US Environmental Protection Agency website; provides baseline built environment and transit accessibility metrics for validation.

domain: Inferring Causal Relationships Between Public Health and the Built Environment, Large-Scale Urban Models and Generative Urban Science, Cross-Scale Urban System Modeling and Spatial Network Analysis
title: Synthetic Urban Population Generation: A Cross-Scale Framework for Evaluating Environmental Health Interventions
brief_rationale: This project develops a high-fidelity synthetic population model to simulate the health impacts of urban policy changes, bridging the gap between aggregate demographic data and individual-level health behaviors.
impact_evidence: Provides urban planners and public health officials with a privacy-preserving, high-resolution simulation tool to evaluate the localized health impacts of built environment interventions, such as green space expansion or active transit zoning, directly supporting 'Health in All Policies' frameworks.
novelty_gap_type: Method and Scale Gap (Bridging aggregate demographic data and individual-level health behaviors for high-resolution causal simulation)
publishability: Computers, Environment and Urban Systems; Health & Place; Journal of Urban Health

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Synthetic individuals nested within census block groups and tracts (cross-scale spatial resolution)
outcomes: Simulated physical activity levels (minutes per week)
Simulated Body Mass Index (BMI)
Simulated respiratory health risk scores
exposures: Proximity to green spaces (meters)
Walkability index (intersection density, land use mix)
Distance to active transit hubs (meters)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE) of built environment interventions on health outcomes, estimated via simulated randomized control trials on the synthetic population using structural causal models.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Spatial Microsimulation (Iterative Proportional Fitting) coupled with Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) Models
robustness_checks: Cross-validation of synthetic population joint distributions against hold-out Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS) data
Sensitivity analysis using alternative spatial weighting matrices (e.g., k-nearest neighbors vs. inverse distance)
Placebo tests applying interventions to theoretically unrelated demographic subgroups
Varying the assumed elasticity parameters between built environment features and health behaviors (low, baseline, high scenarios)
Clustering standard errors at multiple spatial scales (block group, tract, county)
Testing sensitivity to different marginal control totals (1-year vs. 5-year ACS estimates)
expected_tables_figures: Table: Validation metrics of synthetic population (Standardized Root Mean Square Error against PUMS)
Figure: High-resolution spatial heatmaps of baseline vs. post-intervention health risks
Table: CATE estimates of interventions across different socioeconomic subgroups
Figure: Non-linear dose-response curves mapping environmental exposure to health outcomes

data_sources.name: US Census Bureau API (ACS and PUMS)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available via public API, providing aggregate marginal totals and microdata samples without paywalls.

data_sources.name: CDC PLACES Dataset
data_sources.accessibility: Publicly accessible dataset providing model-based population-level estimates for health behaviors and outcomes at the census tract level.

data_sources.name: OpenStreetMap (OSM) via Overpass API
data_sources.accessibility: Open-source, community-driven mapping data freely accessible for extracting street networks, green spaces, and building footprints.

domain: Inferring Causal Relationships Between Public Health and the Built Environment, Large-Scale Urban Models and Generative Urban Science, Cross-Scale Urban System Modeling and Spatial Network Analysis
title: Network Topology of Food Deserts and Its Causal Link to Metabolic Health Outcomes
brief_rationale: This research models food access as a spatial network problem, using graph theory to identify causal links between grocery store proximity and long-term metabolic health metrics in urban centers.
impact_evidence: This research provides actionable evidence for urban planners and public health officials by demonstrating how structural network isolation, rather than mere Euclidean distance, drives metabolic health disparities. Findings can directly inform targeted zoning policies, infrastructure investments, and grocery store subsidies, aligning with high-traction fields like Environmental Health, Medicine, and Public Policy.
publishability: Health & Place (ideal for spatial health research); Social Science & Medicine (strong fit for public health and built environment intersections); Computers, Environment and Urban Systems (matches the spatial network and graph theory methodology).

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Census Tracts in top 50 US Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs) over a 10-year panel (2013-2023).
outcomes: Prevalence of Type 2 Diabetes (tract-level percentage)
Prevalence of Adult Obesity (tract-level percentage)
exposures: Network-based food access centrality (average shortest path on the street network to nearest supermarkets)
Binary treatment indicator for the opening or closing of a major supermarket within a 15-minute walking network radius
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) using a staggered Difference-in-Differences (DiD) design, exploiting the quasi-random timing of supermarket openings and closings across different neighborhoods over the 10-year period.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Staggered Difference-in-Differences (e.g., Callaway & Sant'Anna estimator) with Two-Way Fixed Effects (tract and year).
robustness_checks: Event-study specification to formally test the parallel trends assumption prior to store openings/closings.
Placebo tests using non-metabolic health outcomes (e.g., dental visits or hearing loss) that should not be affected by food access.
Alternative network distance thresholds (e.g., 10-minute vs. 20-minute walking network distance).
Comparison of network-based distance exposure versus traditional Euclidean (straight-line) distance exposure.
Spatial autocorrelation adjustments using Conley spatial standard errors.
Leave-one-MSA-out cross-validation to ensure results are not driven by a single anomalous metropolitan area.
Inclusion of time-varying neighborhood demographic controls (e.g., median rent, income) to account for concurrent gentrification.
expected_tables_figures: Table 1: Summary statistics of tract-level demographics, network topology metrics, and health outcomes.
Figure 1: Spatial network map illustrating food access topology and structural isolation in a sample MSA.
Figure 2: Event-study plot showing pre- and post-treatment effects of supermarket openings on diabetes prevalence.
Table 2: Main staggered DiD regression results with varying fixed effects and controls.
Table 3: Robustness checks table including placebo outcomes, alternative thresholds, and Euclidean vs. Network comparisons.

data_sources.name: CDC PLACES (Population Level Analysis and Community Estimates)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available via the CDC website and API, providing tract-level estimates for chronic disease measures including diabetes and obesity without paywalls.

data_sources.name: USDA Food Environment Atlas / Historical SNAP Retailer Locator
data_sources.accessibility: Publicly accessible federal data providing historical locations and opening/closing dates of SNAP-authorized retailers and supermarkets.

data_sources.name: OpenStreetMap (OSM)
data_sources.accessibility: Open-source, freely accessible spatial database providing comprehensive street network data required for topological routing and graph theory metrics.

data_sources.name: US Census Bureau American Community Survey (ACS) API
data_sources.accessibility: Freely accessible API providing tract-level socioeconomic and demographic control variables (e.g., income, race, vehicle access).

title: Assessing the Impact of Zoning Policy Changes on Urban Compactness via Causal Inference and Satellite Time-Series
brief_rationale: Employs synthetic control methods on longitudinal land-cover data to evaluate the effectiveness of urban density policies in metropolitan corridors.
impact_evidence: Demonstrating the causal effect of zoning policies such as urban growth boundaries or upzoning on actual physical compactness provides critical feedback for urban planners aiming for sustainable, low-carbon cities. It bridges the gap between policy intent and physical reality, offering empirical evidence for climate-resilient urban design.
publishability: Computers, Environment and Urban Systems; Landscape and Urban Planning; Journal of the American Planning Association

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: Metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) and 1km x 1km grid cells over a 20-year annual panel.
outcomes: Urban compactness index
Built-up area density
Spatial fragmentation index
exposures: Binary treatment indicator for the implementation year of specific zoning policy changes (e.g., urban growth boundaries, transit-oriented development upzoning)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT). The strategy relies on the Synthetic Control Method (SCM) to construct a counterfactual for treated cities using a donor pool of untreated cities, assuming parallel trajectories in the pre-treatment period conditional on matched covariates.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Synthetic Control Method (SCM) and Generalized Synthetic Control
robustness_checks: In-space placebo tests assigning treatment to untreated units in the donor pool
In-time placebo tests shifting the treatment year backwards to check for anticipation effects
Leave-one-out sensitivity analysis iteratively dropping cities from the donor pool
Alternative compactness metrics such as Moran's I versus simple density
Varying the pre-treatment matching window length
Controlling for time-varying covariates like local GDP and population growth
Alternative estimators including Staggered Difference-in-Differences (Callaway & Sant'Anna)
expected_tables_figures: Treatment vs. Synthetic Control trend lines pre and post-treatment
Gap plot showing the difference between treated and synthetic control over time
Placebo distribution plot with permutation test p-values
Table of pre-treatment covariate balance between treated and synthetic units
Map of the study area showing treated units and donor pool weights

data_sources.name: National Land Cover Database (NLCD) / ESA WorldCover
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available via public API and bulk download without paywalls, providing longitudinal satellite-derived land cover classifications.

data_sources.name: National Zoning and Land Use Database
data_sources.accessibility: Publicly accessible repository of municipal zoning records and enactment dates, freely available for academic use.

data_sources.name: US Census Bureau API
data_sources.accessibility: Free, public API providing demographic and economic covariates at the MSA and tract levels without manual approval.

title: Quantifying the Synergistic Effects of Urban Vegetation and Albedo Modification on Local Cooling: A GeoAI Simulation
brief_rationale: Uses physics-informed neural networks to optimize spatial placement of cool roofs and trees, filling the need for integrated heat-mitigation strategies.
impact_evidence: Provides actionable, spatially optimized heat mitigation strategies for urban planners and policymakers. By quantifying the non-linear, synergistic cooling effects of combining green infrastructure with cool roofs, this research directly addresses climate adaptation goals, helping to reduce urban heat island (UHI) related mortality, lower peak energy demand, and optimize municipal budgets for climate resilience.
publishability: 1. Computers, Environment and Urban Systems (CEUS) - strong fit for GeoAI, spatial modeling, and urban analytics. 2. Landscape and Urban Planning - ideal for research translating spatial analysis of green infrastructure into urban design. 3. Sustainable Cities and Society - highly relevant for energy, climate adaptation, and urban heat mitigation interventions.

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: High-resolution spatial grid cells (e.g., 30m x 30m) across selected metropolitan areas, analyzed over diurnal summer cycles and extreme heatwave events.
outcomes: Land Surface Temperature (LST)
Estimated Air Temperature (Ta) at 2m
Surface Urban Heat Island Intensity (SUHII)
exposures: Fractional Vegetation Cover (FVC)
Surface Albedo (Roof and Pavement Reflectance)
Interaction Term: FVC x Surface Albedo (Synergistic Exposure)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Marginal cooling effect (Average Treatment Effect) of combined vegetation and albedo interventions. The identification strategy relies on a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) trained on observational satellite data to simulate counterfactual scenarios, isolating the causal synergistic effect from baseline UHI drivers by embedding thermodynamic constraints into the loss function.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN) integrated with Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) baseline models
robustness_checks: Alternative spatial resolutions (aggregating grid cells from 30m to 100m and 250m to test Modifiable Areal Unit Problem effects).
Temporal cross-validation (training the model on early summer data and testing on late summer heatwaves).
Placebo tests using non-heatwave, temperate days to ensure the synergistic effects are specific to high-heat conditions.
Inclusion of spatial lag and spatial error terms in baseline empirical models to account for unobserved spatial autocorrelation.
Alternative albedo and vegetation measurement thresholds (e.g., varying the NDVI threshold for dense vs. sparse vegetation).
Controlling for local wind speed, wind direction, and elevation to isolate advection and topographical effects.
Leave-one-city-out cross-validation to test the generalizability of the GeoAI model across different urban morphologies and climate zones.
expected_tables_figures: Table of marginal cooling effects (in degrees Celsius) for isolated vs. combined interventions.
Spatial heatmaps comparing observed LST with simulated counterfactual LST under optimal vegetation/albedo placement.
Non-linear response curves showing LST reduction as a function of varying vegetation-to-albedo ratios.
Feature importance plots and 2D partial dependence plots illustrating the interaction effect between FVC and Albedo.

data_sources.name: Landsat 8/9 and Sentinel-2 Imagery (via Google Earth Engine)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely accessible via Google Earth Engine API; provides global, high-resolution multispectral data required for calculating LST, NDVI, and surface albedo without paywalls.

data_sources.name: OpenStreetMap (OSM) Building Footprints
data_sources.accessibility: Open-source and freely accessible via the Overpass API; provides detailed vector data for urban morphology, building density, and street networks.

data_sources.name: ERA5-Land (Copernicus Climate Change Service)
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available public dataset providing hourly meteorological data (temperature, wind, radiation) at a 9km resolution, essential for boundary conditions in physics-informed models.

title: Leveraging Multimodal Satellite Fusion and Graph Neural Networks for Predicting Gentrification Dynamics
brief_rationale: Uses time-series Sentinel-2 imagery and socioeconomic census data to model neighborhood change, filling the gap in early-warning systems for displacement risk.
impact_evidence: This research provides a highly scalable, early-warning system for urban planners and municipal governments to identify neighborhoods at high risk of gentrification and displacement. By utilizing globally available satellite imagery and spatial network modeling, policymakers can proactively target affordable housing interventions and anti-displacement policies before housing costs peak, offering significant value to both academic urban studies and practical city management.
publishability: Computers, Environment and Urban Systems; Landscape and Urban Planning; ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

quantitative_specs.unit_of_analysis: US Census Tracts across top 50 Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs) over a 10-year panel (e.g., 2013-2023).
outcomes: Binary gentrification indicator (e.g., tract was in the bottom 40th percentile of median income at baseline, but experienced above-MSA-average growth in rent and bachelor's degree attainment)
Continuous gentrification intensity index (composite score of changes in median rent, property values, and demographic shifts)
exposures: Changes in built environment indices derived from Sentinel-2 (e.g., Normalized Difference Built-up Index - NDBI)
Changes in urban green space (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index - NDVI)
GNN-derived spatial network embeddings (capturing spillover effects and proximity to rapidly changing neighboring tracts)
quantitative_specs.estimand_and_strategy: Predictive risk probability (classification) and marginal predictive contribution of physical environment changes to socio-economic displacement risk. The strategy relies on a spatiotemporal predictive framework where historical satellite and demographic features predict future gentrification status, assuming spatial dependence is captured via the graph structure.
quantitative_specs.model_family: Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Networks (ST-GNN) with baseline comparisons to Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) models and Random Forests.
robustness_checks: Alternative definitions of gentrification (e.g., varying the baseline income threshold from 40th to 50th percentile)
Varying the spatial adjacency matrix (e.g., k-nearest neighbors vs. distance-based thresholds vs. shared boundaries)
Temporal out-of-sample validation (e.g., training on 2013-2018 data to predict 2018-2023 changes)
Spatial cross-validation (leave-one-MSA-out to test model generalizability across different urban morphologies)
Placebo tests using randomly permuted spatial networks to ensure GNN performance gains are driven by true spatial topology
Ablation studies excluding specific satellite indices (e.g., removing NDVI or NDBI) to test feature sensitivity
expected_tables_figures: Table 1: Summary statistics of baseline demographics and satellite-derived features across gentrifying vs. non-gentrifying tracts.
Table 2: Predictive performance metrics (Accuracy, AUC-ROC, F1-Score, Precision-Recall AUC) comparing ST-GNN against baseline models.
Figure 1: Spatial maps of predicted vs. actual gentrification risk for selected case study MSAs.
Figure 2: Feature importance plots (e.g., SHAP values) illustrating the predictive weight of satellite vs. census variables.
Figure 3: GNN attention weight visualizations mapped onto urban geography to show spatial spillover directions.

data_sources.name: US Census Bureau American Community Survey (ACS) 5-Year Estimates API
data_sources.accessibility: Completely free and publicly accessible via the Census API; provides comprehensive tract-level socioeconomic and housing data without paywalls.

data_sources.name: Sentinel-2 Multispectral Instrument (MSI) via Google Earth Engine
data_sources.accessibility: Freely available for research purposes through the Google Earth Engine catalog; provides high-resolution (10m) global optical imagery with scriptable API access.

data_sources.name: OpenStreetMap (OSM) via Overpass API
data_sources.accessibility: Free, open-source, and community-driven mapping data; easily accessible via API for extracting urban road networks and points of interest to construct spatial graphs.
